title: Beyond the Paycheck: What’s Behind America’s Marriage Divide?
gist: Rachel Sheffield argues, drawing on a new Heritage Foundation paper, that the class divide in U.S. marriage is driven less by declining working-class male wages (which have recovered to ~50-year highs) than by rising material expectations for marriage, cultural shifts, and government policies that weaken work incentives.

insights.claim: Young men's median earnings reached a roughly 50-year high in 2025 even as marriage rates kept falling, undercutting the thesis that declining male wages explain marital decline.
insights.speaker: Rachel Sheffield
insights.so_what: If wages aren't the binding constraint, wage-subsidy or job-creation policy alone won't reverse the marriage decline — reframes the policy lever set.
insights.quote: In 2025, young men’s median earnings were at a roughly 50-year high, but marriage rates kept sinking.

insights.claim: Scott Winship of AEI found today's young men generally out-earn the married single-earner fathers of the 1960s-70s era when marriage rates were far higher, directly contradicting the 'marriageability' wage argument made by Oren Cass.
insights.speaker: Rachel Sheffield
insights.so_what: Names the analytic dispute (Winship vs. Cass) for readers tracking the conservative policy debate on family economics.
insights.locator: Examining this question further, Scott Winship

insights.claim: Per the General Social Survey, perceived job stability and ~40-hour median work weeks for at-or-below-median-income young men have held steady or improved since the 1970s, so job-quality deterioration cannot explain the marriage decline either.
insights.speaker: Rachel Sheffield
insights.so_what: Removes a second economic scapegoat, pushing the causal weight toward cultural and policy factors.
insights.locator: According to the General Social Survey (GSS), men’s perceived job stability

insights.claim: Material requirements for marriage have risen sharply: in 1970 only ~a quarter of men at the median first-marriage age (23) owned a home versus nearly 60% in 2023 (at median age 30), and 1970 homes averaged four rooms versus six today.
insights.speaker: Rachel Sheffield
insights.so_what: Suggests marriage 'feels' unaffordable due to lifestyle inflation, not actual decline — a demand-side cultural shift rather than an income shortfall.
insights.quote: in the past, people entered marriage with fewer resources
insights.locator: The median age at first marriage for men in 1970 was 23

insights.claim: Men at age 30 in 1970 were about as likely to be homeowners as married 30-year-old men today, but their homes had fewer rooms and more occupants — a like-for-like comparison showing today's married men are materially better off.
insights.speaker: Rachel Sheffield
insights.so_what: Controls for marriage-age shift, strengthening the 'expectations rose, not means fell' conclusion.
insights.quote: Men at age 30 in 1970 were about as likely to be homeowners as married men today at age 30, but their homes had fewer rooms and more occupants.
insights.locator: To account for the shift in median marriage age

insights.claim: Housing costs explain only recent, not long-term, marriage decline: the share of income a median-earning young man needs for a median mortgage was relatively stable for ~four decades before rising sharply in the last few years.
insights.speaker: Rachel Sheffield
insights.so_what: Bounds the housing-affordability narrative — relevant for whether housing policy can move the marriage needle beyond the recent cohort.
insights.quote: Housing costs may help explain recent marital declines but not the long-term trend.
insights.locator: Is it possible that higher housing prices

insights.claim: Prime-age male labor-force participation fell from 97% in 1955 to 89% in 2024, with many non-participating men reporting they are simply not interested in working — a connection-to-work decline distinct from a wage decline, abetted by looser disability-program criteria.
insights.speaker: Rachel Sheffield
insights.so_what: Links welfare/disability program design to male labor detachment and reduced marriageability — a fiscal-policy channel into family formation.
insights.quote: The share of prime-age men in the labor force fell from 97% in 1955 to 89% in 2024.
insights.locator: While men’s earnings have not declined

insights.claim: Lower relationship stability (higher divorce, no-fault divorce, cohabitation) raises the premium on achieving financial independence before marrying, and unwed births create family complexity that makes later stable marriage harder — disproportionately affecting the working class.
insights.speaker: Rachel Sheffield
insights.so_what: Identifies a self-reinforcing instability loop that policy targeting wages alone cannot break.
insights.quote: unwed births also create family complexity, making it harder to form stable marriages later
insights.locator: Broader cultural changes that have reduced marriage
